facts.fact: PostgreSQL has achieved a 15% average performance improvement per major version on the Join Order Benchmark over the past decade
PostgreSQL tail latency has dropped nearly 50% in the last 10 years (version 8 to version 16)
Cardinality estimation errors can introduce errors of many orders of magnitude, while cost models can introduce errors of at most 30%
A 2024 systematic study found that traditional cost models still often outperform state-of-the-art learned cost models on join ordering, access path selection, and physical operator selection tasks
Adaptive Cost Models dynamically optimize CPU- and I/O-related plan cost parameters at database runtime, showing 20% end-to-end latency improvement on TPC-H benchmark
For a MySQL Top-3 query using window functions, pre-fetch and post-processing optimization reduced response time from 8 seconds to 10 milliseconds (800x improvement)
PostgreSQL's autovacuum daemon runs ANALYZE automatically when approximately 1050 rows have been modified (default threshold: 50 + 10000 * 0.1) for a 10,000-row table
Covering indexes eliminate table lookups by including all query columns in the index, significantly reducing disk I/O
Bitmap index scans read all relevant index rows first, then populate a TID bitmap for sequential table I/O, avoiding random access patterns
Hash joins require the smaller table's hash table to fit in work_mem; if not, PostgreSQL builds the hash in multiple batches and spills to disk
Merge joins are the only practical strategy for joining tables larger than available work_mem, but they require both inputs to be sorted first
PostgreSQL's pg_hint_plan extension allows tweaking execution plans via SQL comments without core language changes
Index seeks are only possible if the first column of a composite index is present in WHERE or JOIN clauses
Sequential scans are often the fastest access method for tables under ~10KB, even with available indexes
Filter pushdown can filter out approximately 50% of traffic to object stores in cloud deployments
PostgreSQL 17 introduces an improved statistics model making cardinality estimation more manageable for very large databases
Left-deep join trees have 2^(n-1) possible join orders for N tables, while bushy trees have exponentially more combinations: 2^(n-1) * C(n-1)
shared_buffers should typically be set to 25-40% of available system memory; beyond 40% provides diminishing returns due to OS page cache overlap
work_mem is allocated per sort/hash operation, not per connection; a single query with multiple sorts can use several allocations
SQL Server 2022+ cardinality estimation feedback can improve estimates for repeating expressions that traditional cardinality estimators miss

examples.problem: PostgreSQL query using collation en_US.UTF-8 on path column performed poorly; queries that should use indexes fell back to filters
examples.solution: Changed database default collation to 'C' (binary ordering), eliminating locale-aware string comparison overhead
examples.result: Query execution plan switched from Filter steps to Index Cond, significantly improving performance

examples.problem: Scoreboard query using window functions took seconds; full table scan with sorting was unavoidable
examples.solution: Applied composite index on (user_id, timestamp) matching PARTITION BY and ORDER BY clauses
examples.result: Query transitioned from full table scan with temporary sort to index scan

examples.problem: Top-3 window function query returned results in 8 seconds with full table scan and massive sort buffer
examples.solution: Pre-fetch optimization with indexed partial scans, then post-process window functions on smaller result set

examples.problem: Loading 2 million posts took 8 seconds without index on created_at
examples.solution: Added B-tree index on created_at column

analogies.analogy: Like a weather forecast error that compounds: a small misprediction of temperature cascades into wrong humidity estimates, which then mispredicts rain. Similarly, a 2x error in predicting rows at a join's first input can lead to a 10x error in total predicted rows if the error flows through multiple joins.

Covering indexes are like having a phone book that doesn't just list names and page numbers, but also includes phone numbers and addresses. You never need to look up each person individually in the detailed directory.

Using an index on a small table is like using a detailed map to find a house on a single-street town — you'd spend more time reading the map than just walking down the street. But in a city, the map saves tremendous time.

A hash join is like writing names on a whiteboard and checking them against a list. If there are too many names and the whiteboard fills, you have to erase, write to paper (disk), then reload sections later — each disk operation is much slower than reading from whiteboard memory.

Rather than delivering all mail in a neighborhood to a central office and filtering it there, mail carriers filter at the postal route level. You avoid the cost of transporting, sorting, and moving rejected mail.

Without a predicate that includes the first index column, the optimizer cannot use index seek. It's like having an alphabetized phone book but asking 'find all people who work in tech' — alphabetization doesn't help until you at least have a name prefix.

key_numbers.metric: PostgreSQL autovacuum ANALYZE threshold (10K row table)

key_numbers.context: Below this, index may not be used for non-covering queries
For N tables; bushy trees have exponentially larger search space

key_numbers.metric: PolarDB threshold for greedy join reorder
key_numbers.context: When table count exceeds 9, switches from exhaustive to greedy heuristic

common_misconceptions.misconception: Index seeks are always possible with composite indexes if any key column is in the WHERE clause
common_misconceptions.reality: Index seeks require the FIRST column of the index to be present in WHERE or JOIN clauses. Later columns in a composite index cannot be used for seeks without the leading column.

common_misconceptions.misconception: SELECT COUNT(*) always forces a full table scan
common_misconceptions.reality: PostgreSQL and SQL Server will use the smallest non-clustered index (non-filtered) available, not a full table scan

common_misconceptions.misconception: Larger indexes are always better for query performance
common_misconceptions.reality: Indexes have negative impact on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE operations; every write must update all indexes. Write-heavy workloads can be harmed by over-indexing.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Machine learning cost models will solve cardinality estimation
common_misconceptions.reality: 2024 research shows traditional cost models still outperform state-of-the-art learned models on standard optimization tasks (join ordering, access path selection, operator selection)

common_misconceptions.misconception: ANALYZE must be run manually after every significant data change
common_misconceptions.reality: PostgreSQL's autovacuum daemon automatically runs ANALYZE based on configurable thresholds; manual runs are only needed for non-standard workloads

common_misconceptions.misconception: All queries benefit from indexes on WHERE clause columns
common_misconceptions.reality: If a query returns >10-20% of table rows, a sequential scan is often faster than index+lookup due to random I/O overhead. Index selectivity matters.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Window functions with PARTITION BY always cause full table scans
common_misconceptions.reality: Composite indexes on (partition_column, order_column) can enable index scans instead of full table scans for window functions

common_misconceptions.misconception: Hash joins are always faster than merge joins
common_misconceptions.reality: Hash joins require sufficient work_mem for the smaller table's hash table. Merge joins are necessary for truly large datasets that exceed work_mem.

common_misconceptions.misconception: Query hints can replace proper schema and index design
common_misconceptions.reality: Hints are temporary stopgaps; they may lose relevance after database upgrades and don't address root causes like missing statistics or poor cardinality estimation

common_misconceptions.misconception: Setting shared_buffers to 50%+ of system memory will improve performance
common_misconceptions.reality: Beyond 40% allocation, diminishing returns occur due to overlap with OS page cache; double-caching data becomes inefficient
